knowledge.what_it_is: An ML Engineer is responsible for designing, developing, and deploying machine learning models into production environments, ensuring they are scalable, efficient, and reliable. They work closely with data scientists to translate complex algorithms into deployable code.
what_you_will_learn: Machine learning pipelines — designing and implementing workflows for model training, testing, and deployment
Model serving architectures — choosing between RESTful APIs, gRPC, or message queues for model exposure
Containerization and orchestration — using Docker, Kubernetes, or other tools to manage model deployments
Model monitoring and logging — setting up metrics, logs, and alerts to track model performance and detect issues
Explainability techniques — applying methods like SHAP, LIME, or feature importance to understand model decisions
Hyperparameter tuning strategies — using Bayesian optimization, grid search, or random search to optimize model performance
Model versioning and deployment — managing multiple versions of models and deploying them efficiently
Data preprocessing and augmentation techniques — applying transformations like normalization, scaling, or data augmentation

knowledge.what_it_is: An ML Engineer focuses on building and deploying machine learning models into production, ensuring they are scalable, efficient, and reliable. In contrast, an AI Engineer works on developing intelligent systems that can learn from data, reason, and interact with humans.
what_you_will_learn: Key differences between ML and AI: — understanding the distinct goals and applications of machine learning versus artificial intelligence
ML vs AI skill sets: — identifying the specific skills required for each role, including programming languages, frameworks, and tools
Model deployment strategies: — learning about containerization (e.g., Docker), orchestration (e.g., Kubernetes), and serving layers (e.g., TensorFlow Serving)
Scalability considerations: — understanding how to handle large datasets, distributed computing, and parallel processing for ML models
Explainability techniques: — familiarizing with methods for interpreting and visualizing ML model outputs, such as SHAP values, LIME, or saliency maps
Model monitoring and maintenance: — learning about strategies for tracking model performance, detecting drift, and updating models in production

knowledge.what_it_is: A Machine Learning Engineer is responsible for designing, developing, and deploying predictive models to solve complex business problems. They work with data scientists to translate business requirements into actionable insights and collaborate with software engineers to integrate ML models into production-ready systems.
what_you_will_learn: Model selection — choosing the right algorithm (e.g., linear regression, decision trees, random forests) for a given problem type
Data preprocessing pipelines — designing efficient data cleaning, feature engineering, and transformation workflows using tools like Pandas, NumPy, and Scikit-learn
Hyperparameter tuning — using techniques like grid search, random search, or Bayesian optimization to optimize model performance with libraries like Hyperopt or Optuna

Model evaluation metrics — understanding and applying metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, AUC-ROC, AUC-PR for classification tasks, and mean squared error, mean absolute error for regression tasks

Model deployment strategies — designing and implementing scalable, fault-tolerant architectures using containerization (e.g., Docker), orchestration tools (e.g., Kubernetes), and cloud platforms (e.g., AWS SageMaker, Google Cloud AI Platform)
Explainability techniques — applying methods like SHAP values, LIME, or feature importance to provide insights into model decisions
Model monitoring and maintenance — setting up continuous integration/continuous deployment pipelines to track model performance, detect drift, and update models as needed

knowledge.what_it_is: In machine learning, matrices are used to represent data and operations on them are essential for tasks like linear regression, neural networks, and dimensionality reduction. Matrices can be represented as two-dimensional arrays of numbers with rows representing features and columns representing samples.
what_you_will_learn: Matrix addition — element-wise addition of corresponding elements in two matrices
Scalar multiplication — multiplying a matrix by a scalar value to scale its elements
Matrix multiplication — dot product of rows of the first matrix with columns of the second matrix, used for neural network layers and linear regression
Transpose operation — swapping rows and columns of a matrix, used for calculating covariance matrices and inverse operations
Determinant calculation — scalar value representing the scaling factor of a matrix's volume, used in linear algebra and eigenvalue decomposition
Inverse matrix computation — finding the matrix that, when multiplied by the original matrix, gives the identity matrix, used in linear regression and neural network initialization
Eigenvalue decomposition — decomposing a matrix into its eigenvectors and eigenvalues, used for dimensionality reduction and feature extraction

Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) — factorizing a matrix into three matrices representing its left-singular vectors, singular values, and right-singular vectors, used for image compression and recommendation systems

knowledge.what_it_is: Scalars, vectors, and tensors are fundamental mathematical concepts used in machine learning to represent data. Scalars are single numbers, while vectors and tensors are multi-dimensional arrays used to store and manipulate complex data.
what_you_will_learn: Scalar operations — understanding basic arithmetic operations on scalars (addition, subtraction, multiplication, division)
Vector representation — representing data as vectors in high-dimensional spaces using techniques like one-hot encoding
Tensor operations — performing element-wise and matrix multiplications on tensors using libraries like NumPy or TensorFlow
Matrix-vector product — understanding the efficient computation of matrix-vector products using optimized algorithms
Linear transformations — applying linear transformations to vectors and tensors using matrices and tensor contractions
Vector norms and distances — computing vector norms (L1, L2) and distances (Euclidean, Manhattan)
Tensor contraction — understanding the process of contracting indices in a tensor to reduce its dimensionality
Broadcasting and reshaping — efficiently manipulating tensors using broadcasting and reshaping operations

knowledge.what_it_is: Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) is a factorization technique used to decompose a matrix into three matrices: U, Σ, and V. It is widely used in machine learning for dimensionality reduction, feature extraction, and data denoising.
what_you_will_learn: Matrix singular values — the square roots of the eigenvalues of A^T A or AA^T, representing the amount of variance explained by each principal component
Left-singular vectors (U) — orthonormal columns that span the column space of A, used for dimensionality reduction and feature extraction
Diagonal matrix Σ — contains the singular values in descending order, used to compute the rank of a matrix and determine the number of non-zero singular values
Right-singular vectors (V) — orthonormal columns that span the row space of A, used for data denoising and image compression
SVD algorithm — computing U, Σ, and V using various methods such as power iteration, QR decomposition, or randomized SVD
Computing SVD in practice — choosing between different algorithms and techniques, such as truncated SVD, partial SVD, or incremental SVD for large datasets
Interpreting SVD results — understanding the meaning of singular values, left-singular vectors, and right-singular vectors in the context of a specific problem

knowledge.what_it_is: In linear algebra, the determinant and inverse of a matrix are essential concepts used to solve systems of equations, find the volume of parallelepipeds, and compute the adjugate and cofactor matrices. In machine learning, these concepts are crucial for tasks such as data normalization, feature scaling, and regularization.
what_you_will_learn: Determinant calculation — using expansion by minors, cofactor expansion, or LU decomposition to find the determinant of a matrix
Inverse of a matrix — using Gaussian elimination, LU decomposition, or the Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse to compute the inverse of a square matrix
Adjugate and cofactor matrices — understanding how these matrices are related to the determinant and inverse of a matrix
Matrix rank and nullity — using determinants to find the rank and nullity of a matrix, which is essential for tasks such as data normalization
Singular value decomposition (SVD) — understanding how SVD relates to the determinant and inverse of a matrix, and its applications in machine learning
Condition number — computing the condition number of a matrix using determinants or other methods, which is crucial for regularization techniques

knowledge.what_it_is: Eigenvalues and diagonalization are fundamental concepts in linear algebra used to analyze and transform matrices, crucial for many machine learning algorithms.
what_you_will_learn: Eigenvalue decomposition — factorizing a matrix into its eigenvalues and eigenvectors, useful for spectral clustering and dimensionality reduction

Diagonalization of symmetric matrices — transforming a symmetric matrix into a diagonal matrix using its eigenvalues and eigenvectors, essential for principal component analysis (PCA) and kernel methods
Power iteration method — an iterative algorithm to approximate the dominant eigenvalue and corresponding eigenvector of a matrix, used in PageRank and community detection

QR algorithm — a method to compute the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of a matrix by iteratively applying QR decompositions, widely used for eigenvalue decomposition and singular value decomposition (SVD)
Jacobi eigenvalue algorithm — an iterative method to diagonalize a symmetric matrix using Jacobi rotations, often used in conjunction with QR algorithm for robustness

Eigendecomposition of covariance matrices — analyzing the eigenvectors and eigenvalues of covariance matrices to understand data distribution and correlations, crucial for feature selection and dimensionality reduction

knowledge.what_it_is: Linear Algebra is a fundamental mathematical discipline that deals with vector spaces, linear transformations, and matrices. It provides a framework for solving systems of equations, finding eigenvalues and eigenvectors, and understanding the geometry of high-dimensional spaces.
what_you_will_learn: Vector Spaces — abstract spaces that consist of vectors, equipped with operations such as addition and scalar multiplication
Linear Transformations — functions between vector spaces that preserve linear combinations and scalar multiplication
Matrices — rectangular arrays of numbers used to represent linear transformations and solve systems of equations
Determinants — a scalar value associated with a square matrix that can be used to determine the invertibility of the matrix
Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors — scalars and vectors that, when multiplied by a matrix, result in the same vector scaled by the eigenvalue
Linear Independence — the property of a set of vectors being able to express any other vector as a linear combination
Span and Basis — concepts used to describe the structure of a vector space and find a minimal set of vectors that span it

knowledge.what_it_is: In machine learning, gradients, Jacobians, and Hessians are essential mathematical concepts used to optimize model parameters and understand the behavior of complex functions.
what_you_will_learn: Gradient descent — an optimization algorithm that updates model weights by minimizing the loss function using gradient information
Jacobian matrix — a square matrix containing first-order partial derivatives of a multivariate function, used for sensitivity analysis and hyperparameter tuning
Hessian matrix — a square matrix containing second-order partial derivatives of a multivariate function, used to determine local minima and maxima in optimization problems
Chain rule for gradients — a mathematical formula for computing the gradient of a composite function using the gradients of its individual components
Backpropagation — an efficient algorithm for computing gradients of complex neural network architectures using the chain rule and matrix multiplication
Computing Jacobian matrices using automatic differentiation libraries (e.g., TensorFlow, PyTorch) — leveraging software tools to compute derivatives in high-dimensional spaces
Visualizing Hessian matrices using eigenvalue decomposition or singular value decomposition — understanding the structure of the Hessian to inform optimization strategies
subtopics: optimization algorithms (stochastic gradient descent, adam, rmsprop)
hyperparameter tuning techniques (grid search, random search, bayesian optimization)

knowledge.what_it_is: In machine learning, derivatives and partial derivatives are used to optimize model parameters by finding the gradient of a loss function with respect to each parameter. This is crucial for training deep neural networks.
what_you_will_learn: Gradient Descent — an optimization algorithm that uses the derivative of the loss function to update model weights
Backpropagation — a method for computing the derivatives of the loss function with respect to each model weight using chain rule and partial derivatives
Chain Rule of Differentiation — a mathematical concept used in backpropagation to compute the derivative of composite functions
Partial Derivatives — computing the derivative of a function with respect to one or more variables, essential for understanding how neural networks learn from data
Multivariate Calculus — extending calculus to multiple variables and functions, necessary for understanding neural network optimization techniques
Hessian Matrix — a square matrix used in second-order optimization methods like Newton's method to compute the curvature of the loss function
Automatic Differentiation — a technique that uses the chain rule and partial derivatives to compute gradients without manual implementation
Gradient Clipping — a regularization technique used to prevent exploding gradients during training by clipping the norm of the gradient vector

knowledge.what_it_is: The chain rule of derivation is a fundamental concept in calculus used to compute the derivative of composite functions, essential for optimizing machine learning models.
what_you_will_learn: Composite function derivatives — understanding how to apply the chain rule to complex functions, such as sigmoid(x) * x
Derivative of exponential and logarithmic functions — mastering the rules for computing derivatives of e^x, ln(x), and log(x)
Product rule and quotient rule in calculus — applying these rules to compute derivatives of products and quotients of functions
Chain rule application in machine learning — understanding how to apply the chain rule to compute gradients of loss functions with respect to model parameters
Gradient computation using automatic differentiation — using libraries like TensorFlow or PyTorch to compute gradients automatically
Derivative of trigonometric functions — computing derivatives of sin(x), cos(x), and tan(x)
Higher-order derivatives in machine learning — understanding how to compute higher-order derivatives for Hessian matrix calculation

knowledge.what_it_is: Python is a high-level, interpreted programming language widely used in machine learning and data science due to its simplicity, readability, and extensive libraries.
what_you_will_learn: Indentation — understanding Python's syntax for block-level indentation and its importance in code organization
Variables and Data Types — declaring variables with `x = 5` or `x = 'hello'`, understanding built-in types like `int`, `float`, `str`, `list`, `dict`, `set`, and `bool`
Operators and Control Flow — using arithmetic, comparison, logical, and assignment operators, controlling flow with `if-else`, `for`, `while`, and `try-except` statements
Functions — defining reusable blocks of code with `def func(x): return x * 2`, understanding default arguments, keyword-only arguments, and variable-length argument lists
Modules and Packages — importing modules like `numpy`, `pandas`, and `matplotlib` using `import numpy as np`, creating custom packages with `__init__.py` files
Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) Concepts — classes, objects, inheritance, polymorphism, encapsulation, and abstraction using `class Car: def __init__(self): ...`
File Input/Output — reading and writing text files with `open()` function, CSV files with `pandas.read_csv()`, JSON files with `json.load()`
Regular Expressions (regex) — pattern matching with `re.search()`, `re.match()`, and `re.sub()` functions, understanding character classes, groups, and anchors
List Comprehensions and Generators — concise list creation with `[x**2 for x in range(10)]`, generator expressions with `(x**2 for x in range(10))`
Lambda Functions — small anonymous functions with `lambda x: x**2`, understanding their use cases and limitations
Decorators — modifying function behavior with `@decorator_name` syntax, creating custom decorators with `def my_decorator(func): ...`
Async/Await — asynchronous programming with `async def func(): await asyncio.sleep(1)`, using `await` to wait for coroutines to complete

knowledge.what_it_is: Statistics is a fundamental branch of mathematics that deals with the collection, analysis, interpretation, presentation, and organization of data. In machine learning, statistics provides the mathematical framework for understanding and modeling complex relationships between variables.
what_you_will_learn: Descriptive Statistics — summarizing and describing the basic features of a dataset using measures such as mean, median, mode, variance, and standard deviation
Inferential Statistics — making inferences about a population based on a sample of data, including hypothesis testing and confidence intervals
Probability Distributions — understanding and working with probability distributions such as Bernoulli, Binomial, Poisson, Normal (Gaussian), Exponential, and Gamma
Bayesian Inference — using Bayes' theorem to update probabilities based on new evidence, including Bayesian linear regression and Bayesian neural networks
Hypothesis Testing — testing a null hypothesis about a population parameter, including t-tests, ANOVA, and non-parametric tests
Confidence Intervals — constructing intervals that estimate a population parameter with a specified level of confidence
Regression Analysis — modeling the relationship between a dependent variable and one or more independent variables using linear regression, logistic regression, and polynomial regression
Time Series Analysis — analyzing and forecasting time series data using techniques such as ARIMA, SARIMA, and exponential smoothing
Anomaly Detection — identifying unusual patterns in data using methods such as Z-score, IQR, and One-class SVM
Principal Component Analysis (PCA) — reducing dimensionality by transforming correlated variables into uncorrelated ones
K-Means Clustering — grouping similar data points into clusters based on their features
Linear Regression Assumptions — understanding the assumptions of linear regression, including linearity, independence, homoscedasticity, and normality

knowledge.what_it_is: Probability theory provides a mathematical framework for modeling uncertainty and randomness, which is essential in machine learning to make predictions and decisions under uncertainty.
what_you_will_learn: Random Variables — mathematical representations of uncertain quantities with a well-defined set of possible values
Probability Distributions — functions that assign probabilities to the possible values of random variables (e.g. Bernoulli, Binomial, Poisson)
Conditional Probability — probability of an event given that another event has occurred (e.g. Bayes' theorem)
Independence and Dependence — concepts for modeling relationships between random variables
Expectation and Variance — measures of central tendency and spread of a random variable's distribution
Law of Large Numbers — the average of many independent and identically distributed random variables converges to their population mean
Central Limit Theorem — the distribution of sample means approaches a normal distribution as sample size increases

knowledge.what_it_is: Bayes' theorem is a mathematical formula that describes the probability of an event occurring given some prior knowledge and new evidence. It's widely used in machine learning to update probabilities based on new data.
what_you_will_learn: Conditional probability — understanding how Bayes' theorem updates probabilities based on new evidence
Prior probability — calculating the initial probability of an event before observing any new data
Likelihood function — defining the probability of observing new data given a specific model or hypothesis
Posterior distribution — updating the prior probability using Bayes' theorem to obtain the final probability estimate
Bayesian inference — applying Bayes' theorem to make predictions, classify data, or estimate parameters in machine learning models
Gaussian mixture models — using Bayes' theorem to model complex distributions as a weighted sum of simpler Gaussian components
Variational Bayesian methods — approximating posterior distributions using optimization techniques and Bayes' theorem

knowledge.what_it_is: Random variances and probability density functions (PDFs) are fundamental concepts in machine learning used to model uncertainty and make predictions about continuous random variables.
what_you_will_learn: Probability distributions — understanding the different types of distributions (normal, uniform, exponential, etc.) and their properties
Cumulative distribution function (CDF) vs. probability density function (PDF) — understanding how CDFs and PDFs are related and used in machine learning
Mean and variance of a random variable — calculating and interpreting the mean and variance of a continuous random variable
Standard deviation and standard error — understanding the difference between standard deviation and standard error, and when to use each
Chebyshev's inequality — applying Chebyshev's inequality to estimate the probability of a random variable falling within a certain range
Law of large numbers (LLN) and central limit theorem (CLT) — understanding how LLN and CLT are used in machine learning to make predictions about sample means and variances

knowledge.what_it_is: In machine learning, a probability distribution describes the likelihood of different outcomes or values in a dataset. Understanding types of distributions is crucial for modeling real-world phenomena and making predictions.
what_you_will_learn: Bernoulli Distribution — a discrete distribution for binary outcomes (e.g., coin flips), parameterized by a single probability
Binomial Distribution — an extension of Bernoulli, used for multiple trials with a fixed probability of success
Poisson Distribution — models the number of events occurring in a fixed interval, often used for count data
Normal Distribution (Gaussian) — a continuous distribution for real-valued outcomes, parameterized by mean and variance
Exponential Distribution — models the time between events in a Poisson process, often used for waiting times or lifetimes
Weibull Distribution — a flexible distribution for modeling failure times or lifetimes with varying shapes
Gamma Distribution — a two-parameter distribution for modeling rates or proportions with shape and scale parameters
Beta Distribution — models the probability of success in a Bernoulli trial, often used for Bayesian inference

knowledge.what_it_is: Descriptive statistics is a set of methods used to summarize and describe the basic features of a dataset, providing insights into its central tendency, variability, and distribution. It is essential for understanding data quality, identifying patterns, and making informed decisions in machine learning.
what_you_will_learn: Mean — calculating the average value of a dataset using various methods (arithmetic mean, geometric mean, harmonic mean)
Median — finding the middle value of a dataset when it is sorted in ascending or descending order
Mode — identifying the most frequently occurring value(s) in a dataset
Standard Deviation (SD) — measuring the amount of variation or dispersion from the average value
Variance — calculating the squared differences from the mean, providing a measure of spread
Interquartile Range (IQR) — finding the difference between the 75th and 25th percentiles to describe data variability
Skewness — measuring the asymmetry of a distribution using various methods (Pearson's coefficient, Fisher-Pitman test)
Kurtosis — calculating the peakedness or flatness of a distribution compared to a normal distribution
subtopics: data scaling methods (log transformation, square root transformation)

knowledge.what_it_is: Inferential statistics is a branch of machine learning that deals with making conclusions or predictions about a population based on a sample of data. It involves using statistical models and techniques to estimate population parameters, test hypotheses, and make predictions.
what_you_will_learn: Hypothesis testing — the process of testing a hypothesis about a population parameter using a sample statistic (e.g., t-test, z-test)
Confidence intervals — constructing an interval estimate for a population parameter based on a sample statistic (e.g., confidence interval for mean, proportion)
Regression analysis — modeling the relationship between a dependent variable and one or more independent variables (e.g., linear regression, logistic regression)
ANOVA (Analysis of Variance) — testing the equality of means among multiple groups (e.g., one-way ANOVA, two-way ANOVA)
Non-parametric tests — statistical tests that do not assume a normal distribution in the data (e.g., Wilcoxon rank-sum test, Kruskal-Wallis H-test)
Bayesian inference — using Bayes' theorem to update probabilities based on new evidence (e.g., Bayesian linear regression, Bayesian neural networks)
Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods — simulating complex systems using Markov chains and Monte Carlo integration
Bootstrapping — resampling with replacement to estimate the variability of a statistic or model

knowledge.what_it_is: Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) is a paradigm that organizes code into objects, each with properties and methods, to promote modularity, reusability, and maintainability. In Machine Learning, OOP principles are applied to design robust and efficient models.
what_you_will_learn: Classes — defining custom data types as blueprints for creating objects
Objects — instances of classes with their own attributes (data) and methods (functions)
Inheritance — deriving new classes from existing ones, inheriting properties and behavior
Polymorphism — using same method name with different implementations in subclasses
Encapsulation — hiding internal implementation details within objects to protect data integrity
Abstraction — exposing only necessary information about an object's interface while hiding implementation details
Composition — combining multiple objects to form a new, more complex object
Single Responsibility Principle (SRP) — ensuring each class has a single reason to change
Open-Closed Principle (OCP) — allowing classes to be extended without modifying their source code
Dependency Injection (DI) — decoupling dependencies between objects for better modularity

knowledge.what_it_is: NumPy (Numerical Python) is a library for working with arrays and mathematical operations, widely used in scientific computing and machine learning applications.
what_you_will_learn: Array creation — creating NumPy arrays from lists, tuples, or other arrays using numpy.array()
Basic operations — performing arithmetic operations on arrays (e.g., addition, subtraction, multiplication) using array methods like +, -, *, /
Indexing and slicing — accessing and manipulating array elements using indexing (e.g., arr[0], arr[:3]) and slicing (e.g., arr[1:4])
Broadcasting — performing operations on arrays with different shapes and sizes using numpy's broadcasting rules
Linear algebra functions — using NumPy's linear algebra functions like numpy.linalg.inv(), numpy.linalg.det(), and numpy.linalg.eig() for matrix operations
Random number generation — generating random numbers using numpy.random.rand(), numpy.random.normal(), and other random number distributions
Statistical functions — using NumPy's statistical functions like numpy.mean(), numpy.median(), and numpy.std() for data analysis

knowledge.what_it_is: In machine learning, variables and data types refer to the representation of numerical or categorical values used to train and evaluate models. Understanding these concepts is crucial for efficient model development and deployment.
what_you_will_learn: NumPy arrays — multidimensional containers for numerical data, optimized for performance
Pandas DataFrames — tabular structures for storing and manipulating large datasets
TensorFlow Tensors — multi-dimensional arrays used to represent tensors in machine learning models
PyTorch Tensors — similar to TensorFlow tensors but with a more dynamic and flexible API
Numpy data types (int, float, bool) — fundamental numerical types used for array operations
Pandas data type inference — automatically determining the best data type for a column based on its values
Data normalization techniques (min-max scaling, standardization) — transforming data to improve model generalizability
Categorical encoding methods (one-hot encoding, label encoding) — converting categorical variables into numerical representations

knowledge.what_it_is: Pandas is an open-source, BSD-licensed library providing high-performance data structures and operations for efficient data manipulation and analysis in Python.
what_you_will_learn: DataFrames — two-dimensional tables of data with columns of potentially different types, similar to Excel spreadsheets
Series — one-dimensional labeled array capable of holding any data type, similar to a column in a spreadsheet
Indexing and selecting data — using labels, integers, or boolean arrays to access specific rows and columns
Data merging and joining — combining DataFrames based on common columns or indices using inner, left, right, and outer joins
GroupBy operations — splitting data into groups based on one or more keys and applying aggregation functions
Handling missing data — detecting and filling missing values using various strategies such as mean, median, or interpolation
Data cleaning and preprocessing — removing duplicates, handling outliers, and transforming data types
Pivot tables — reshaping data from long to wide format for easier analysis and visualization

knowledge.what_it_is: Data structures are fundamental concepts in machine learning, enabling efficient storage and manipulation of data. They include arrays, linked lists, stacks, queues, trees, graphs, heaps, hash tables, and sets.
what_you_will_learn: Arrays — storing collections of elements, with indexing and slicing operations
Linked Lists — dynamic arrays with efficient insertion and deletion of elements
Stacks — Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) data structures for parsing expressions and evaluating postfix notation
Queues — First-In-First-Out (FIFO) data structures for job scheduling and message passing
Trees — hierarchical data structures for representing file systems, parse trees, and decision trees
Graphs — non-linear data structures for modeling relationships between objects, with applications in social network analysis and recommendation systems
Hash Tables — efficient data structures for storing key-value pairs, with applications in caching and indexing
Sets — unordered collections of unique elements, with applications in frequency counting and duplicate removal

knowledge.what_it_is: Databases are essential for machine learning as they store and manage large datasets used for training models. SQL databases like MySQL and PostgreSQL are widely used, while No-SQL databases like MongoDB and Cassandra offer flexible schema designs.
what_you_will_learn: SQL vs No-SQL databases — understanding the trade-offs between structured (SQL) and unstructured (No-SQL) data storage
Database normalization — designing tables to minimize data redundancy and improve query performance
Indexing techniques — using primary keys, secondary indexes, and covering indexes to speed up queries
Query optimization — rewriting SQL queries to reduce execution time and resource usage
Data modeling for machine learning — creating database schema that supports efficient querying and data retrieval
Database connections in Python — using libraries like psycopg2 (for PostgreSQL) and mysql-connector-python (for MySQL)
No-SQL databases for ML — understanding the benefits of MongoDB, Cassandra, and other No-SQL options for large-scale datasets

knowledge.what_it_is: Loops are used to iterate over data structures, perform repetitive tasks, and implement control flow in machine learning models.
what_you_will_learn: For loops — iterating over lists, arrays, or other iterable objects using a for loop syntax
While loops — conditional iteration based on a boolean expression, often used for convergence checks or iterative optimization
Nested loops — combining multiple iterations to perform complex tasks, such as matrix multiplication or data augmentation
Looping through dictionaries — accessing and iterating over key-value pairs in Python dictionaries using the .items() method
List comprehensions — concise syntax for creating lists from existing iterables, often used for data preprocessing or feature engineering
Itertools — using the itertools module to implement advanced iteration patterns, such as permutations or combinations
Generator expressions — lazy evaluation of expressions that produce iterables, useful for large-scale data processing or memory-efficient computation

knowledge.what_it_is: Conditional statements are used to control the flow of execution in machine learning models, allowing them to make decisions based on specific conditions.
what_you_will_learn: If-else statements — using Python's if-else syntax to implement basic conditional logic
Switch-case statements — using Python's match statement (introduced in 3.10) for more concise conditional expressions
Logical operators — understanding the use of and, or, not, xor, and other logical operators in conditional statements
Conditional expressions — using the ternary operator to concisely implement simple conditionals
Lambda functions with conditions — creating small anonymous functions that take conditions as input
Map-reduce pattern with conditionals — applying conditional logic to data transformations using map-reduce pipelines

knowledge.what_it_is: Exceptions in machine learning refer to unexpected errors or anomalies that occur during model training, testing, or deployment. These can be caused by various factors such as data quality issues, algorithmic limitations, or hardware constraints.
what_you_will_learn: Try-except blocks — using Python's built-in try-except syntax to catch and handle exceptions in machine learning code
Exception types — understanding the difference between built-in exception classes like ValueError, TypeError, and custom exceptions for specific ML tasks
Error handling strategies — implementing techniques such as retrying failed operations, logging errors, or terminating the model
Custom exceptions — creating tailored exception classes to handle domain-specific error cases in machine learning pipelines
Logging and monitoring — using libraries like Loguru or Prometheus to track and analyze exceptions in ML workflows
Error reporting tools — utilizing services like Sentry or Airbrake to collect and visualize ML-related errors

knowledge.what_it_is: Functions and built-in functions are essential components of Python programming used extensively in machine learning to encapsulate reusable code blocks that perform specific tasks.
what_you_will_learn: Lambda functions — anonymous functions defined using a single expression, often used for simple data transformations
Map function — applies a given function to each item of an iterable (e.g., list, tuple) and returns a new iterable with the results
Filter function — constructs an iterator from elements of an iterable for which a function returns True
Reduce function — applies a rolling computation to sequential pairs of values in a list (e.g., summing all numbers)
Built-in functions: len(), type(), range(), zip(), enumerate() — understanding their usage and application in machine learning pipelines
Higher-order functions: map(), filter(), reduce() — using them to abstract away repetitive code and improve readability

knowledge.what_it_is: Matplotlib is a popular Python data visualization library used extensively in Machine Learning and Data Science to create informative, high-quality visualizations of data. It provides a wide range of tools for creating plots, charts, and graphs, making it an essential tool for any ML practitioner.
what_you_will_learn: Plotting functions — creating line plots, scatter plots, histograms, bar plots, etc., using functions like plot(), scatter(), hist(), bar()
Customizing plots — modifying colors, fonts, labels, titles, and legends to suit specific visualization needs
Axes and subplots — creating multiple plots on a single figure, customizing axis limits, labels, and ticks
Legend and annotation — adding legend entries, annotations, and text labels to plots for better understanding
Saving plots — exporting plots as images (PNG, PDF, SVG), interactive HTML files, or displaying them inline in Jupyter notebooks using tools like matplotlib.pyplot.show()
Interactive visualizations — creating zoomable, panable, and hoverable plots using tools like plotly, bokeh, or mpld3
Color maps and colormaps — selecting and customizing color schemes for plots to effectively convey data insights

knowledge.what_it_is: Seaborn is a Python data visualization library built on top of Matplotlib, used extensively in Machine Learning and Data Science roles to create informative and attractive statistical graphics.
what_you_will_learn: Importing Seaborn — importing the library and its dependencies for use in ML projects
Visualizing Distributions — using distplot() and kdeplot() to visualize data distributions, including histograms and kernel density estimates
Scatterplots with Regression Lines — creating scatterplots with regression lines using regplot() and lmplot() for exploratory data analysis
Heatmaps and Clustermaps — visualizing correlation matrices and clustering results using heatmap() and clustermap() functions
Bar Plots and Box Plots — creating bar plots and box plots to compare categorical and continuous variables using countplot(), barplot(), and boxplot() functions
Pair Plots and Swarm Plots — visualizing relationships between multiple variables using pairplot() and swarmplot() for feature selection and dimensionality reduction
Customizing Visualizations — using style sheets, color palettes, and other customization options to tailor Seaborn plots to specific ML project needs

knowledge.what_it_is: Data sources are the origin of data used to train, validate, and test machine learning models. They can be structured or unstructured, internal or external, and come from various sources such as databases, APIs, files, and more.
what_you_will_learn: CSV files — importing and preprocessing CSV data using pandas in Python
JSON files — loading and parsing JSON data using the json library in Python
Databases — connecting to relational databases like MySQL or PostgreSQL using SQL Alchemy in Python
APIs — consuming RESTful APIs using requests in Python, including authentication and pagination
Web scraping — extracting data from websites using BeautifulSoup and Scrapy in Python
NoSQL databases — working with MongoDB, Cassandra, or Redis using their respective drivers in Python
Data lakes — storing and processing large amounts of unstructured data using Apache Hadoop or Spark in Python
Cloud storage — accessing and processing data stored on cloud platforms like AWS S3 or Google Cloud Storage using their respective SDKs in Python
Kafka topics — consuming and producing data from Apache Kafka topics using the Confluent library in Python

knowledge.what_it_is: The Internet is a global network of interconnected computers and servers that enable data exchange, communication, and information sharing. In Machine Learning, the Internet serves as a vast source of structured and unstructured data, including text, images, audio, and video.
what_you_will_learn: HTTP/2 protocol — an improved version of HTTP for efficient multiplexing and header compression
TCP/IP model — understanding the Transmission Control Protocol/Internet Protocol stack for network communication
IP addresses and subnetting — managing IP address allocation, routing, and addressing in Machine Learning applications
DNS resolution and caching — optimizing domain name system lookups for faster data retrieval
Web scraping techniques using BeautifulSoup and Scrapy — extracting structured data from websites
API design patterns (RESTful, GraphQL) — building scalable and maintainable APIs for data exchange
Data compression algorithms (GZIP, Brotli) — reducing network latency and bandwidth usage
Secure Sockets Layer/Transport Layer Security (SSL/TLS) — encrypting data in transit using certificates and keys

knowledge.what_it_is: An API (Application Programming Interface) is a set of defined rules that enable different software systems to communicate with each other, allowing data exchange between services and applications. In machine learning, APIs are used to fetch data from external sources, integrate models with other tools, and deploy models in production environments.
what_you_will_learn: RESTful API — designing APIs for web services using HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) and resource-based URLs
API Endpoints — defining entry points for data exchange between systems, including GET, POST, PUT, and DELETE operations
API Keys — securing API access with unique identifiers for authentication and authorization
Data Fetching APIs — using libraries like requests or http.client to retrieve data from external sources (e.g., CSV, JSON files)
Model Serving APIs — deploying machine learning models as web services using frameworks like TensorFlow Serving, AWS SageMaker, or Azure Machine Learning
API Gateways — managing API traffic and security with tools like NGINX, Amazon API Gateway, or Google Cloud Endpoints
API Documentation — generating documentation for APIs using tools like Swagger, OpenAPI, or API Blueprint

knowledge.what_it_is: Mobile apps are software applications designed to run on mobile devices, such as smartphones and tablets. In machine learning, mobile apps can be used to collect data from users, provide personalized experiences, and enable real-time decision-making.
what_you_will_learn: Native vs cross-platform development — understanding the trade-offs between platform-specific code and shared codebases
Mobile app frameworks for ML: TensorFlow Lite, Core ML, ONNX Runtime — integrating ML models into mobile apps with optimized performance
Data collection and storage in mobile apps: Firebase Realtime Database, Firestore, SQLite — designing data pipelines to support ML model training
Model deployment on mobile devices: Android Things, iOS Machine Learning Framework — deploying trained models on edge devices for real-time inference
Mobile app security and privacy considerations: encryption, secure data storage, GDPR compliance — ensuring responsible ML development in mobile apps
User experience design for ML-powered mobile apps: visualizations, feedback mechanisms, user interface best practices — creating intuitive interfaces for ML-driven features

knowledge.what_it_is: IoT (Internet of Things) refers to the network of physical devices, vehicles, home appliances, and other items embedded with sensors, software, and connectivity, allowing them to collect and exchange data. In the context of Machine Learning, IoT devices can serve as data sources for training models, enabling applications such as predictive maintenance, anomaly detection, and automation.
what_you_will_learn: Sensor Data — collecting and processing data from various sensors (temperature, humidity, pressure, acceleration, etc.) using protocols like MQTT, CoAP, or HTTP
Device Management — understanding device registration, authentication, and authorization mechanisms for secure IoT operations
Data Preprocessing — handling missing values, outliers, and noisy data in IoT datasets to prepare them for ML model training
Feature Engineering — extracting relevant features from raw sensor data (e.g., time series analysis, signal processing) to improve model performance
Edge Computing — deploying ML models on edge devices or gateways to reduce latency, conserve bandwidth, and improve real-time decision-making
Fog Computing — leveraging cloud-like services at the edge of networks to process IoT data in a decentralized manner
IoT Data Formats — understanding standard formats like CSV, JSON, and Protobuf for storing and transmitting IoT data
MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport) — a lightweight messaging protocol for efficient communication between devices and servers

knowledge.what_it_is: Data formats are standardized ways of representing and storing data, essential for machine learning pipelines to ensure interoperability and reproducibility.
what_you_will_learn: CSV — comma-separated values format, widely used for tabular data, with optional header row and quoting
JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) — lightweight, human-readable format for exchanging structured data between applications, using key-value pairs and arrays
Parquet — columnar storage format optimized for big data analytics, supporting compression, encoding, and efficient querying
Pandas DataFrames — in-memory representation of tabular data, providing efficient data manipulation and analysis capabilities
NumPy Arrays — multi-dimensional array object for efficient numerical computations, with support for various data types and operations
TFRecord — binary format for storing and retrieving TensorFlow datasets, enabling efficient loading and processing of large-scale data
HDF5 (Hierarchical Data Format 5) — self-describing file format for storing large datasets, supporting compression, encryption, and metadata management
Apache Arrow — cross-language development platform for in-memory data processing, providing a common format for data exchange between languages and frameworks
MessagePack — binary-based serialization format for efficient data transfer and storage, with support for various data types and compression algorithms

knowledge.what_it_is: Comma Separated Values (CSV) is a plain text file format used to store tabular data, where each row represents an observation and each column represents a feature. CSV files are widely used as input/output formats for machine learning models.
what_you_will_learn: CSV reader libraries — using pandas.read_csv() or csv.reader() to load CSV files into memory
Data types in CSV — understanding how to specify data types (e.g., int64, float32) when reading CSV files
Header rows and index columns — handling header rows and index columns in CSV files for machine learning tasks
Missing value handling — strategies for dealing with missing values in CSV files, such as imputation or deletion
Data normalization — applying scaling techniques (e.g., StandardScaler) to numerical features in CSV files
Feature selection — using techniques like mutual information or recursive feature elimination to select relevant features from a CSV file

knowledge.what_it_is: Excel is a spreadsheet software developed by Microsoft, widely used for data manipulation and analysis in various industries, including finance, healthcare, and marketing.
what_you_will_learn: PivotTables — summarizing large datasets using drag-and-drop interface, aggregating values with GROUP BY and filtering with Slicer
VLOOKUP and INDEX-MATCH functions — efficiently retrieving data from external tables or sheets using exact matches and wildcards
Conditional Formatting — highlighting trends, outliers, and anomalies in data visualization, including color-coding, icons, and formulas
Data Validation — enforcing rules for user input, such as date ranges, number formats, and dropdown lists, to ensure data quality and consistency
Macros and VBA scripting — automating repetitive tasks using Visual Basic for Applications (VBA), recording and editing macros for efficient workflow optimization

knowledge.what_it_is: JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is a lightweight data interchange format used to represent structured data, commonly used in machine learning pipelines for data preprocessing and model input/output.
what_you_will_learn: JSON schema — defining the structure of JSON data using a schema language, ensuring data consistency and facilitating data validation
JSON parsing libraries — using libraries like json5 or ujson to efficiently parse large JSON datasets in Python
JSON serialization — converting complex Python objects into JSON format for model input/output, using libraries like json.dumps() or pandas.to_json()
JSON deserialization — loading JSON data into Python objects, using libraries like json.loads() or pandas.read_json()
JSON schema validation — using tools like jsonschema to validate JSON data against a predefined schema, ensuring data integrity and consistency
JSON data compression — compressing large JSON datasets using algorithms like gzip or lz4, reducing storage requirements and improving model training times

knowledge.what_it_is: Parquet is an open-source columnar storage format designed to store and process large datasets efficiently, commonly used in big data analytics and machine learning pipelines. It provides features like compression, encoding, and statistical metadata.
what_you_will_learn: Columnar storage — storing data in columns instead of rows for faster querying and aggregation
Compression algorithms — understanding how Parquet uses Snappy, Gzip, LZO, and ZStandard to reduce storage size
Encoding schemes — learning about Bit-Packed, Delta, and RLE encodings used in Parquet
Statistical metadata — analyzing the built-in statistics like min, max, sum, count, and mean stored with each column
Data type support — understanding how Parquet supports various data types like integers, floats, strings, and timestamps
Schema evolution — learning about how to handle changes in schema without losing data or requiring full rewrites
File format specification — understanding the Parquet file structure, including metadata blocks and data pages

knowledge.what_it_is: Other data formats are specialized file formats used to store and exchange machine learning data, including images, audio, video, and text data. These formats provide efficient storage and retrieval of large datasets, enabling faster model training and deployment.
what_you_will_learn: HDF5 — Hierarchical Data Format version 5: a binary format for storing large-scale scientific data, widely used in machine learning for image and audio processing
TFRecord — TensorFlow's binary record file format: stores serialized Protocol Buffers messages, used for efficient storage of model inputs and outputs
ARFF — Attribute-Relation File Format: a text-based format for representing datasets, commonly used for data preprocessing and feature engineering
CSVW — CSV on the Web: an extension to CSV that adds semantic meaning to tabular data, enabling better data integration and exchange
Protobuf — Protocol Buffers: a language-agnostic data serialization format, widely used in machine learning for efficient storage and transmission of model parameters and outputs
MessagePack — A binary-based efficient object serialization library, often used in machine learning for storing and exchanging model inputs and outputs

knowledge.what_it_is: Data cleaning is the process of detecting, correcting, and transforming raw data into a format suitable for machine learning model training. It involves handling missing values, outliers, and inconsistencies to ensure data quality and accuracy.
what_you_will_learn: Handling missing values — strategies such as mean/median imputation, forward/backward fill, and interpolation
Outlier detection and removal — techniques like Z-score, IQR, and density-based methods (e.g., DBSCAN)
Data normalization and scaling — standardization, min-max scaling, and log transformation for feature balancing
Handling categorical data — encoding schemes (e.g., one-hot, label encoding), and handling ordinal variables
Removing duplicates and near-duplicates — techniques like hashing and similarity-based methods
Data preprocessing pipelines — using libraries like Pandas, NumPy, and Scikit-learn to automate cleaning tasks

knowledge.what_it_is: Feature engineering is the process of selecting, transforming, and creating new features from existing ones to improve the performance of machine learning models.
what_you_will_learn: Feature selection — techniques for choosing the most relevant features, such as mutual information, recursive feature elimination, and filter methods (e.g., correlation analysis)
Feature extraction — methods for transforming existing features into new ones, including principal component analysis (PCA), independent component analysis (ICA), and autoencoders
Dimensionality reduction — techniques for reducing the number of features while preserving important information, such as t-SNE, UMAP, and feature hashing
One-hot encoding — converting categorical variables into numerical representations using binary vectors
Label encoding — assigning a unique integer label to each category in a categorical variable
Scikit-learn's SelectFromModel — selecting features based on their importance scores from a trained model
Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE) — iteratively removing the least important features until a specified number of features is reached
Permutation feature importance — evaluating the importance of each feature by randomly permuting its values and measuring the decrease in model performance

knowledge.what_it_is: Feature scaling and normalization are essential preprocessing techniques in machine learning to ensure that all features have similar magnitudes, preventing feature dominance and improving model performance. This process transforms raw data into a common scale, making it easier for algorithms to learn from.
what_you_will_learn: Standardization — subtracting the mean and dividing by standard deviation to center the data around zero with unit variance
Normalization — scaling features to a common range (e.g., 0-1) using techniques like Min-Max Scaler or Robust Scaler
Log Scaling — applying logarithmic transformation to reduce skewness and improve model interpretability
StandardScaler in scikit-learn — using the StandardScaler class for standardization with mean centering and variance scaling
MinMaxScaler in scikit-learn — using the MinMaxScaler class for normalization with feature range scaling
Robust Scaler in scikit-learn — using the RobustScaler class for robust normalization with interquartile range scaling
Feature scaling techniques for categorical features — one-hot encoding, label encoding, or binary encoding to preserve meaningful information
Handling missing values during feature scaling and normalization — imputation methods like mean, median, or mode substitution
subtopics: feature scaling techniques for categorical features
handling missing values during feature scaling and normalization

knowledge.what_it_is: Dimensionality reduction is a technique used to reduce the number of features or dimensions in a dataset while retaining as much information as possible, making it easier to visualize and analyze high-dimensional data.
what_you_will_learn: Principal Component Analysis (PCA) — a widely-used linear dimensionality reduction method that projects high-dimensional data onto lower-dimensional subspaces using orthogonal eigenvectors
t-SNE — a non-linear dimensionality reduction technique for visualizing high-dimensional data in a lower-dimensional space, often used for clustering and visualization
Autoencoders — neural networks that learn to compress and reconstruct input data, useful for unsupervised feature learning and dimensionality reduction

Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) — a linear algebra technique for decomposing matrices into three factors: left-singular vectors, singular values, and right-singular vectors, used in PCA and other applications
Feature selection using mutual information — a method for selecting the most informative features based on their mutual information with the target variable
Kernel PCA — an extension of traditional PCA that uses kernel methods to project data onto higher-dimensional feature spaces
t-Distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding (t-SNE) variants — such as t-SNE-PP, t-SNE-ITK, and UMAP, which offer improved performance and scalability for large datasets

knowledge.what_it_is: Feature selection is the process of selecting a subset of relevant features from a larger set to improve model performance, reduce overfitting, and increase interpretability. It's essential in machine learning as high-dimensional data often contains irrelevant or redundant features that can negatively impact model accuracy.
what_you_will_learn: Filter methods — techniques for selecting features based on statistical measures such as mutual information, correlation coefficients, and chi-squared tests
Wrapper methods — algorithms that evaluate the performance of a model on different feature subsets using metrics like cross-validation and permutation importance
Embedded methods — techniques that learn feature weights or importance scores during model training, such as L1/L2 regularization and recursive feature elimination
Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE) — an embedded method for selecting features based on their contribution to the model's performance
Correlation-based feature selection — a filter method for selecting features with high correlation coefficients with the target variable
Mutual Information-based feature selection — a filter method for selecting features with high mutual information with the target variable
Permutation Importance — an embedded method for evaluating feature importance by permuting feature values and measuring the impact on model performance
Feature Scoring — techniques for assigning scores to features based on their relevance, such as recursive feature elimination and permutation importance

knowledge.what_it_is: Supervised learning is a type of machine learning where an algorithm learns from labeled training data to make predictions on new, unseen data. It's called 'supervised' because the model is trained with input-output pairs and must learn to map inputs to correct outputs.
what_you_will_learn: Regression — predicting continuous values (e.g., house prices) using linear or non-linear models like Linear Regression, Decision Trees, Random Forests
Classification — predicting categorical labels (e.g., spam vs. not spam emails) using algorithms like Logistic Regression, Support Vector Machines, K-Nearest Neighbors
Overfitting and Underfitting — understanding the trade-off between model complexity and training data size to prevent overfitting and underfitting
Regularization Techniques — L1, L2 regularization, dropout, early stopping to prevent overfitting and improve generalization
Hyperparameter Tuning — using techniques like Grid Search, Random Search, Bayesian Optimization to optimize model performance for specific datasets
Model Evaluation Metrics — understanding metrics like Mean Squared Error (MSE), Mean Absolute Error (MAE), R-Squared, Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1 Score for evaluating model performance
Ensemble Methods — combining multiple models (e.g., Bagging, Boosting) to improve predictive accuracy and robustness
Feature Engineering — selecting and transforming relevant features from raw data using techniques like dimensionality reduction, feature scaling, and normalization

knowledge.what_it_is: Unsupervised learning is a type of machine learning where algorithms identify patterns and relationships within data without prior knowledge of the correct output. It's used to discover hidden structures, group similar items, or generate new data.
what_you_will_learn: K-Means Clustering — partitioning data into K clusters based on similarity, with initialization methods like random or k-means++
Hierarchical Clustering — building a tree-like structure to represent hierarchical relationships between data points, with linkage methods like single, average, and complete
DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise) — identifying clusters based on density and proximity, with parameters epsilon and min_samples
Principal Component Analysis (PCA) — reducing dimensionality by projecting data onto a lower-dimensional space using eigenvectors and eigenvalues
Autoencoders — learning compact representations of data by reconstructing input from encoded features, with encoder-decoder architecture and loss functions like MSE or cross-entropy
t-SNE (t-Distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding) — visualizing high-dimensional data in a lower-dimensional space using t-distribution and gradient descent
Clustering Evaluation Metrics — understanding metrics like silhouette score, calinski-harabasz index, and davies-bouldin index to evaluate clustering quality

knowledge.what_it_is: Semi-supervised learning (SSL) is an approach to machine learning where a small amount of labeled data is combined with a large amount of unlabeled data to improve model performance. This technique is particularly useful when labeled data is scarce or expensive to obtain.
what_you_will_learn: Self-training — using pre-trained models on unlabeled data to fine-tune the model's weights and improve its generalization
Co-training — training multiple models simultaneously, each using a different subset of features, to leverage the strengths of each model
Tri-training — combining self-training with co-training to further improve model performance by leveraging multiple views of the data
Multi-view learning — representing the same data in multiple feature spaces and combining the predictions from each view to improve overall accuracy
Generative adversarial training (GANs) for semi-supervised learning — using GANs to generate synthetic labeled data that can be used to augment the existing labeled dataset
Pseudo-labeling — assigning labels to unlabeled data based on the model's confidence and using these pseudo-labeled samples to fine-tune the model
Consistency regularization — adding a consistency loss term to the training objective to encourage the model to produce consistent predictions when given different views of the same input

knowledge.what_it_is: Self-supervised learning is an approach to machine learning where models learn to represent data without explicit supervision or labeled examples, instead relying on self-generated or auxiliary tasks to improve performance.
what_you_will_learn: Contrastive Loss — a technique for maximizing agreement between positive pairs and minimizing agreement between negative pairs
Triplet Loss — an extension of contrastive loss that considers the relationship between three samples (anchor, positive, and negative)
Autoencoders — neural networks that learn to compress and reconstruct input data in an unsupervised manner
Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) — a framework for learning generative models through adversarial training
Self-Supervised Learning with Multi-Task Learning — using auxiliary tasks to improve performance on the primary task without explicit supervision
Instance Discrimination — a technique for self-supervised learning where models learn to distinguish between different instances of the same class
Temporal Ensembling — an ensemble method that combines predictions from multiple models trained on different subsets of the data
Noise Contrastive Estimation (NCE) — a technique for estimating probabilities using noise-contrastive estimation

knowledge.what_it_is: Reinforcement learning is a type of machine learning where an agent learns to take actions in an environment to maximize a reward signal. It's commonly used in robotics, game playing, and autonomous systems.
what_you_will_learn: Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) — mathematical framework for modeling decision-making under uncertainty
Q-learning — model-free reinforcement learning algorithm for estimating action values using Q-function
Deep Deterministic Policy Gradients (DDPG) — actor-critic method for continuous action spaces and high-dimensional observations
Policy Gradient Methods (PGMs) — family of algorithms for policy optimization in reinforcement learning
Value Iteration Networks (VINs) — neural network architecture for approximating value functions and planning
Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) — model-free algorithm for training policies using trust region methods
Actor-Critic Methods — combination of policy gradient and value function estimation for efficient exploration-exploitation trade-off

knowledge.what_it_is: Data loading refers to the process of importing and preparing data into a machine learning model, typically from external sources such as CSV files, databases, or APIs. This step is crucial for training and testing models.
what_you_will_learn: Pandas — reading and writing CSV, Excel, and other file formats using read_csv(), to_csv()
NumPy arrays — loading data into memory-efficient arrays using loadtxt(), genfromtxt()
CSV readers — using csv.reader or pandas.read_csv for efficient parsing of large datasets
Database connections — connecting to SQL databases using sqlite3, psycopg2, mysql-connector-python
API calls — making HTTP requests to fetch data from APIs using requests library
Data cleaning and preprocessing — handling missing values, outliers, and data normalization using pandas.DataFrame.dropna(), sklearn.preprocessing.StandardScaler

knowledge.what_it_is: Splitting data into training and testing sets to evaluate model performance, preventing overfitting and ensuring generalizability.
what_you_will_learn: Data Splitting — dividing dataset into train (70-80%), validate (10-20%), and test sets (10%) for hyperparameter tuning and final evaluation
Stratified Sampling — sampling from imbalanced datasets to maintain class distribution in both training and testing sets
KFold Cross Validation — splitting data into k subsets, using each subset as a test set while training on the remaining k-1 subsets
Leave-One-Out (LOO) Cross Validation — training model on all data except one instance, evaluating on that instance
Holdout Method — reserving a fixed portion of data for testing and training on the remaining dataset
Data Augmentation — generating new training instances by applying transformations to existing ones, reducing overfitting
Random Sampling — randomly selecting instances from the entire dataset for training and testing
Shuffle Split — shuffling the dataset before splitting it into train and test sets to avoid any bias

knowledge.what_it_is: Data preparation is the process of transforming raw data into a format suitable for machine learning model training, including handling missing values, encoding categorical variables, scaling/normalizing numerical features, and removing irrelevant or redundant information.
what_you_will_learn: Handling Missing Values — strategies for dealing with missing data, including imputation methods (mean, median, mode), interpolation, and deletion
Data Encoding — techniques for converting categorical variables into numerical representations, such as one-hot encoding, label encoding, and hash encoding
Feature Scaling/Normalization — methods for rescaling numerical features to a common range, including standardization, normalization, and min-max scaling
Data Preprocessing Pipelines — using libraries like Pandas, NumPy, and Scikit-learn to create efficient data preprocessing workflows
Handling Outliers — techniques for identifying and addressing outliers in the data, such as winsorization, trimming, and robust scaling
Feature Selection/Engineering — strategies for selecting or creating relevant features from raw data, including correlation analysis, recursive feature elimination, and polynomial transformations

knowledge.what_it_is: Model selection is the process of choosing the best machine learning model for a given problem, considering factors such as accuracy, complexity, and interpretability. It involves evaluating multiple models and selecting the one that performs best on a validation set.
what_you_will_learn: Cross-validation — splitting data into training and testing sets to evaluate model performance
Grid search — systematically searching for optimal hyperparameters using a grid of possible values
Random search — randomly sampling hyperparameter combinations to optimize model performance
Model selection metrics — understanding metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, AUC-ROC, and mean squared error
Overfitting vs underfitting — recognizing when models are too complex or too simple for the problem at hand
Regularization techniques — applying L1, L2, or dropout regularization to prevent overfitting
Early stopping — stopping training when model performance on a validation set starts to degrade
Ensemble methods — combining multiple models to improve overall performance

knowledge.what_it_is: Model tuning, also known as hyperparameter tuning, is the process of finding the optimal combination of model parameters that result in the best performance on a given dataset. It's an essential step in machine learning pipeline to improve model accuracy and robustness.
what_you_will_learn: Grid Search — a brute-force approach for searching the optimal hyperparameter values by iterating over all possible combinations
Random Search — a more efficient alternative to grid search, where random subsets of hyperparameters are sampled from the prior distribution
Bayesian Optimization — a probabilistic approach that uses Bayesian inference to model the objective function and optimize hyperparameters
Hyperband — an efficient algorithm for tuning hyperparameters using a combination of random search and sequential model-based optimization
Gradient-Based Methods — techniques such as gradient descent and its variants (e.g., Adam, RMSProp) can be used for hyperparameter tuning by backpropagating the gradients through the hyperparameters
Early Stopping — a technique to prevent overfitting by monitoring the validation loss during training and stopping when it starts to increase
Learning Rate Schedulers — adaptive methods (e.g., step, cosine, plateau) that adjust the learning rate during training based on the model's performance
Ensemble Methods — combining multiple models with different hyperparameters to improve overall performance

knowledge.what_it_is: Prediction is a fundamental task in machine learning where an algorithm generates predictions based on input data, often using statistical models or neural networks.
what_you_will_learn: Regression — predicting continuous values (e.g., house prices) using linear regression, decision trees, random forests, or support vector machines
Classification — predicting categorical labels (e.g., spam vs. not spam emails) using logistic regression, decision trees, random forests, or neural networks
Supervised Learning — training models on labeled data to predict target variables
Unsupervised Learning — identifying patterns in unlabeled data for clustering, dimensionality reduction, or anomaly detection
Model Evaluation Metrics — understanding metrics such as mean squared error (MSE), mean absolute error (MAE), R-squared, accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score
Hyperparameter Tuning — using techniques like grid search, random search, or Bayesian optimization to optimize model performance
Regularization Techniques — applying L1, L2, or elastic net regularization to prevent overfitting
Ensemble Methods — combining multiple models (e.g., bagging, boosting) for improved prediction accuracy

knowledge.what_it_is: Classification is a type of supervised machine learning where you train a model to predict categorical labels or class memberships from input data, such as image classification (e.g., cats vs. dogs), sentiment analysis (positive/negative reviews), or spam detection.
what_you_will_learn: Binary Classification — models that output two possible classes (e.g., spam/not spam, cancer/no cancer)
Multi-Class Classification — models that output one of multiple classes (e.g., image classification with 10 animal categories)
One-vs-All (OVA) and One-vs-One (OvO) Scoring — strategies for handling multi-class problems
Logistic Regression — a linear model for binary classification, predicting probabilities using logistic function
Decision Trees — tree-based models that recursively partition data to predict class labels
Random Forests — ensemble methods combining multiple decision trees for improved accuracy and robustness
Support Vector Machines (SVMs) — kernel-based models finding the optimal hyperplane separating classes in high-dimensional space
K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) — instance-based models predicting class labels based on nearest neighbors' votes
Gradient Boosting Machines (GBMs) — ensemble methods iteratively combining weak models to produce strong predictions

knowledge.what_it_is: K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) is a supervised learning algorithm used for classification and regression tasks, where new data points are predicted based on their similarity to existing data points.
what_you_will_learn: Distance metrics — Euclidean distance, Manhattan distance, Minkowski distance: choosing the right metric for KNN
K value selection — understanding how to choose the optimal number of nearest neighbors (k) for a given problem
Weighting schemes — uniform weighting, inverse distance weighting, and other techniques for combining neighbor votes
Nearest neighbor search algorithms — Ball Tree, KD-Tree, and other data structures for efficient KNN searches
KNN variants — using weighted KNN, adaptive KNN, or other modifications to improve performance
Handling missing values — strategies for dealing with missing features in the training dataset
Hyperparameter tuning — techniques for optimizing KNN hyperparameters such as k and weighting schemes

knowledge.what_it_is: Linear Regression is a supervised learning algorithm used for predicting continuous outcomes based on one or more predictor variables. It assumes a linear relationship between the target variable and the predictors, making it suitable for problems where the relationship is not too complex.
what_you_will_learn: Cost Function — calculating the mean squared error (MSE) or mean absolute error (MAE) between predicted and actual values
Gradient Descent — optimizing model parameters using iterative updates based on partial derivatives of the cost function with respect to each parameter
Linear Model Equation — representing the relationship between predictors and target variable as a linear combination of coefficients and features
Coefficient Estimation — calculating optimal values for model coefficients using least squares or maximum likelihood estimation methods
Feature Scaling — normalizing predictor variables to have zero mean and unit variance to improve model convergence and prevent feature dominance
Regularization Techniques — applying L1 (Lasso) or L2 (Ridge) regularization to reduce overfitting by penalizing large coefficients
Model Evaluation Metrics — using metrics like R-squared, mean squared error (MSE), or mean absolute error (MAE) to assess model performance and select the best hyperparameters

knowledge.what_it_is: Logistic regression is a supervised learning algorithm used to predict binary outcomes (0/1, yes/no) based on one or more predictor variables. It models the probability of an event occurring using a logistic function.
what_you_will_learn: Binary classification — understanding how logistic regression is used for binary outcome prediction
Sigmoid function — learning the mathematical representation of the logistic function (1 / (1 + e^(-z)))
Cost function — calculating the mean squared error between predicted probabilities and actual outcomes using cross-entropy loss
Gradient descent — optimizing model weights to minimize cost function using backpropagation
Regularization techniques — applying L1, L2, or ElasticNet regularization to prevent overfitting and improve generalizability
Hyperparameter tuning — selecting optimal values for learning rate, number of iterations, and regularization strength using grid search or random search
Feature scaling — normalizing predictor variables to ensure equal importance in model predictions
Logistic regression vs. linear regression — understanding key differences between the two algorithms

knowledge.what_it_is: Polynomial regression is a type of supervised learning algorithm that models complex relationships between input variables and target outputs using polynomial functions, often used when linear regression fails to capture non-linear patterns.
what_you_will_learn: Polynomial features — transforming data into higher-dimensional space using polynomial terms (e.g., x^2, x^3) for capturing non-linear interactions
Regularization techniques — applying L1 and L2 regularization to prevent overfitting in polynomial regression models
Kernel trick — mapping input data onto a higher-dimensional feature space using kernel functions (e.g., Gaussian RBF), enabling efficient computation of polynomial features
Polynomial degree selection — determining the optimal degree of the polynomial function based on cross-validation and model evaluation metrics
Feature scaling — normalizing input variables to improve convergence of gradient descent optimization algorithms in polynomial regression
Regularization hyperparameter tuning — selecting optimal regularization strengths (e.g., alpha, lambda) for L1 and L2 regularization
Model selection criteria — evaluating and comparing different polynomial regression models using metrics such as mean squared error (MSE), R-squared, and cross-validation scores

knowledge.what_it_is: A Support Vector Machine (SVM) is a supervised learning algorithm that finds the hyperplane maximizing the margin between classes, used for classification and regression tasks.
what_you_will_learn: Kernel trick — transforming data into higher-dimensional space to linearize non-linearly separable problems
Soft margins — allowing some training errors in exchange for better generalization performance
Support vectors — identifying the most informative instances that define the decision boundary
Margin maximization — finding the optimal hyperplane that maximizes the distance between classes
Regularization techniques — using L1, L2, or elastic net regularization to prevent overfitting
SVM types — understanding the differences between C-SVC, ν-SVC, and one-vs-all/one-vs-rest SVMs
Choosing kernel parameters — selecting optimal values for kernel type, degree, and gamma
Handling high-dimensional data — using techniques like PCA or feature selection to reduce dimensionality
